title: utf8::is_valid() returns false for valid ASCII strings
author: jakoch

title: Add conversion funcs between UTF16 and UTF32
author: ModulePillow
body:
Hello, author(s).
Firstly, thanks for your work helping other coders!

I believe it's necessary to integrate utf16<->utf32 conversion functions.

Reasons:
Though UTF8 is the center of this repository, adding u16tou32 and u32tou16 enhances the versatility.

Especially on Windows, UTF-16 is widely used (aka. wchar_t on Win), and an app frequently converts UTF-16 to other encodings.

Comments:
Dear author(s), if you support this issue, I can create a pull request to add these features.

title: Suggestion for substring
author: GitSparTV

title: String with invalid first byte not sanitized
author: petewarden
body:
First, thanks for this great library, I appreciate your hard work!

I'm running into an issue where my strings sometimes have bogus first bytes, for example 0xd9. The `is_valid()` and `replace_invalid()` functions seem to think this is valid, but it causes a crash in Android's `NewStringUTF()` JNI function.

I don't have a full repro project for you, but here's the input I plugged into my version of the [test_replace_invalid](https://github.com/nemtrif/utfcpp/blob/master/tests/test_checked_api.h#L188) test:

```c++
    char invalid_sequence[] = "\xd9\x85\xd8\x80\xe0\xa0\xc0\xaf\xed\xa0\x80z";
```

The test passes, but if I'm understanding UTF-8 encoding correctly, this isn't valid? Let me know if I'm missing something!

title: Release tag on 4.0.9 is incorrect.
author: heitbaum
body: https://github.com/nemtrif/utfcpp/tree/v4.09 I believe it should be https://github.com/nemtrif/utfcpp/tree/v4.0.9

title: Compilation error due to method assert clashing with standard macro
author: yachoor
body:
Latest version (4.0.7) introduces a static assert helper for pre-c++11 compilers with a method named `assert`. Unfortunately it clashes with standard `assert` macro whenever cassert or assert.h is included, causing compilation failures: https://gcc.godbolt.org/z/sxP7Gxe69

Would you consider renaming it or at least surrounding declaration and usage with parentheses to prevent macro expansion?

title: Bad C++ version detection in Visual Studio
author: rconde01
body:
utfcpp uses `__cplusplus` macro to determine C++ version, but Visual Studio doesn't update this value by default, therefore utfcpp doesn't detect the version correctly.

See https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cpp/build/reference/zc-cplusplus?view=msvc-170

title: size() and length()
author: RolandHughes
body:
Just found this and am glancing through.  Did not see "Discussions" so adding "issue."

size() and length() for std::string will just return the number of bytes. Does this library have an encoding aware length() even if it had to have different name?

Yes, theoretically one could convert the string to UTF-32 or a std::basic_string<> where the CharT was 32-bit and get a somewhat accurate character count provided one doesn't have any [UTF-64](https://utf64.moreplease.com). Seems to me though, if the library is already walking through the bytes to go to the next size larger UTF, it should be able to just count without creating the new string.

Been looking for something to replace CopperSpice strings with in [this project](https://github.com/RolandHughes/ls-cs). Will never use C++20 with that project for [very real reasons](https://lscs-software.com/LsCs-Manifesto.html).

title: Warnings from -Wsign-conversion on Gcc 11.4.0 with C++ 20
author: benblan
body:
Hello,

when I include the library (v4.0.5) in my code, I get the 2 following warnings. Not sure it comes from my code. Maybe the way I include it?
```
/home/project/extern/utfcpp/utf8/core.h: In instantiation of ‘utf8::internal::utf_error utf8::internal::get_sequence_4(octet_iterator&, octet_iterator, utf8::utfchar32_t&) [with octet_iterator = const char*; utf8::utfchar32_t = char32_t]’:
/home/project/extern/utfcpp/utf8/core.h:277:53:   required from ‘utf8::internal::utf_error utf8::internal::validate_next(octet_iterator&, octet_iterator, utf8::utfchar32_t&) [with octet_iterator = const char*; utf8::utfchar32_t = char32_t]’
/home/project/extern/utfcpp/utf8/core.h:305:45:   required from ‘utf8::internal::utf_error utf8::internal::validate_next(octet_iterator&, octet_iterator) [with octet_iterator = const char*]’
/home/project/extern/utfcpp/utf8/core.h:437:79:   required from ‘octet_iterator utf8::find_invalid(octet_iterator, octet_iterator) [with octet_iterator = const char*]’
/home/project/extern/utfcpp/utf8/core.h:447:28:   required from here
/home/project/extern/utfcpp/utf8/core.h:237:20: warning: conversion to ‘unsigned int’ from ‘int’ may change the sign of the result [-Wsign-conversion]
  237 |         code_point += (utf8::internal::mask8(*it) << 6) & 0xfff;
      |         ~~~~~~~~~~~^~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
/home/project/extern/utfcpp/utf8/core.h: In instantiation of ‘utf8::internal::utf_error utf8::internal::get_sequence_3(octet_iterator&, octet_iterator, utf8::utfchar32_t&) [with octet_iterator = __gnu_cxx::__normal_iterator<const char*, std::__cxx11::basic_string<char> >; utf8::utfchar32_t = char32_t]’:
/home/project/extern/utfcpp/utf8/core.h:274:53:   required from ‘utf8::internal::utf_error utf8::internal::validate_next(octet_iterator&, octet_iterator, utf8::utfchar32_t&) [with octet_iterator = __gnu_cxx::__normal_iterator<const char*, std::__cxx11::basic_string<char> >; utf8::utfchar32_t = char32_t]’
/home/project/extern/utfcpp/utf8/core.h:305:45:   required from ‘utf8::internal::utf_error utf8::internal::validate_next(octet_iterator&, octet_iterator) [with octet_iterator = __gnu_cxx::__normal_iterator<const char*, std::__cxx11::basic_string<char> >]’
/home/project/extern/utfcpp/utf8/core.h:437:79:   required from ‘octet_iterator utf8::find_invalid(octet_iterator, octet_iterator) [with octet_iterator = __gnu_cxx::__normal_iterator<const char*, std::__cxx11::basic_string<char> >]’
/home/project/extern/utfcpp/utf8/core.h:452:59:   required from here
/home/project/extern/utfcpp/utf8/core.h:218:20: warning: conversion to ‘unsigned int’ from ‘int’ may change the sign of the result [-Wsign-conversion]
  218 |         code_point += (*it) & 0x3f;
      |         ~~~~~~~~~~~^~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
```

title: CMake could be added to documentation
author: rexkogitans
body:
Maybe my issue sounds quite stupid, but I am struggling to get UTF8CPP working with CMake. In the file `CMakeLists.txt`, I added these two lines:

```
find_package(utf8cpp REQUIRED)
include_directories(/usr/include/utf8cpp)
```

Then I can use
```C++
#include <utf8.h>
```

This is working, but hardcoding the include directories cannot be the right way to go. There is variable called `utf8cpp_DIR` which is set to `/usr/share/utf8cpp/cmake`. This directory includes 3 files which contain CMake rules, but I am not able to figure out how to use it.

I kindly ask to drop some lines about how to use CMake to build a project which uses UTF8CPP in the documentation.

body:
Hello
I wonder if this library provides a way to convert case to folded/upper/lower? Or to compare strings with insensible case?
If not, what would be the recommended way? ICU provides such a feature, but it is huge

title: Strings are one character short with version 3.2.5 through 4.0.0
author: afh
body:
When compiling [ledger](https://ledger-cli.org) with utfcpp version 3.2.5 until 4.0.0 (4.0.1 fixes the issue) there is an issue where a string passed from ledger's Python module to its C++ native code is shortened by one character (see [`ledger/ledger#2302`](https://github.com/ledger/ledger/issues/2302) for details).

In ledger's test suite this results in data files not being found as their filepath is truncated, e.g. `test/regress/4D9288AE.dat` becomes `test/regress/4D9288AE.da`.

Currently the ledger project is trying to figure out the best way forward and any insight folks more familiar with utfcpp can provide would be greatly appreciated.

A few thoughts / questions:
* Has a similar or related issue been raised with utfcpp in the past?
* What information would be helpful to you in order to get a better understanding of how utfcpp is (mis?)used in ledger?
